author: @SirArghPirate
text: I believe the reason for all the gear is to make shifting more reliable. Having only two gears, the smallest and largest would make the chain slip easily and wear faster because the jump between them would be so large.

author: @bonzobanana1
text:
I've been saying this for a long time that many bikes are over-geared. High end road bikes I think can have to 12 cogs on the cassette and 2 at the front, a few gears cancel themselves out as duplicated and others you can't use because of the chainline. It's not better for going up hill or fast downhill as you can still have a 11T cog on a 7 speed cassette and maybe a 34T at the low end of gearing. Many of these high end cassettes are drilled to save weight and made of hardened aluminium but still they wear out far faster than the cheap steel cassettes because they are aluminium cogs and they are very thin. The chains are thinner to allow for thinner cog spacing and the accuracy of the derailleur and lubrication becomes more critical as you are dealing with much more narrow tolerances between the cogs. Many bikes now have electronic shifting which needs perhaps monthly charging (don't forget). If you do the bike has less gears than other bikes because it will stick in its last gear until you charge it I believe.

Cycling at the high end is a fashion industry, having the same bike as the pro racers is bragging rights etc. Expensive bikes, expensive parts, expensive frequent maintenance, huge diminishing returns compared to cheaper bikes. A good quality Claris road bike can be got for £500 (2x8), a bike that can perform 99% as well as a £10k bike is about £2k or less. The most dangerous component on a bike is a carbon fibre fork, they can fail without warning meaning a face plant at 30mph with other vehicles going over you. It's rare of course but far more likely than a steel fork which pretty much never fail and give ample warning of failure.

The 3 speed sturmey archer hub you could ride in any weather as internal, great for folding bikes as you couldn't knock or damage it compared to derailleurs on small wheels. You could get them with coaster brakes so the rear brake was pretty much maintenance free and worked in all weathers and didn't have to run a brake cable to the rear. Ultra reliable too as simple engineering. You get 70 year old shed bikes where the gears still work fine once you've replaced or oiled the shifter cable.

Just to be fair I should point out that many who buy these high end bikes claim they can optimise their power more efficiently by having a wider selection of gears and you get crisp fast gear changes as the gears are so tightly spaced. It can be a nice user experience and you go a little faster. 

As ever though the sensible value choice in bikes is neither cheapest or most expensive its the bike that hasn't been compromised by low price but doesn't have vanity components. I would say a Claris road bike and others who are more performance focused would probably Say Shimano 105 equipped bike.

author: @Kiyarose3999
text: You are thinking about you and your needs, then say ‘’why would anyone want it’’, doesn’t make sense as your needs are different to others, who do benefit from having 18-21 gears. A cyclist can’t peddle up a steep’ish hill or go 25-30 mph without having both a tiny gear and a big gear, which means the others ‘step’ gears are needed in between.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no I wasn't - I was talking about assessing your own needs and matching them

author: @RaperJason
text:
All down to personal choice I use everything from single to 27  single great for fixies, BMX, Trials, Jump bike etc. 3 speed SA  good for average short reliable runs Mid wifes and District Nurses loved them I tried a London 15 mile commute and never again give me back my 8 speed 🤣. Try pedaling up bike park wales with to few gears and you will ruin your day by being worn out unless you are super super  fit and even then you will do less runs and waste your entry fee
Down hill with techy bits will use every gear you can get on a 12 speed 
The 21/24/27 sets are the industry trying to make one bike fit all from fit to knackered and from road to off road and I personally think that in the price range they produce good reliable bikes for they are doing a sterling job look at the cost of a Sturmey Archer compared to a good derailleur set up £££ saved
Compared to the pile of dangerous rubbish I got for my 10th birthday modern bikes are fantastic (It was a Chopper Heavy, unstable, awful brakes,bad gear ratios and it woud loop out at the hint of a wheelie Cost £35 the equivelent of nearly £1000. Today I can get a Specialized Rock Hopper for £450 much better value and lots of gears 🤣

author: @kom2falcon
text:
LOL.  Well Robert, the KOM in my screen name stands for King of the Mountain,  which is awarded to the best fastest bicycle climber in the race.  So just like a sports car with a 6 or 8 speed transmission it's all about trying to stay in your power band RPMs.
In cycling it's your pedaling cadence both as a strength power band and breathing power band depending on the rider and what they are best at.  
I liked to stay at about 155 heart beats per minute and about 16 MPH going up the mountain and would flip gears until I was there.  On a 80 mile race you want all the energy you can save up for the sprint finish.
I agree for most people it isn't needed but it does have it's purpose.
For your E trike you are correct you don't need more than a 2 speed really so go with what's best for you.
But I had to take up for cyclists and their reasons for cassette choices.  Why we choose to suffer so much is a different story.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - oops!! - I don't mean to offend mate but I am sure you know that - I rode seriously for the Halifax star wheelers and after a bit I ditched all of the gears and used to ride on a single freewheel - the different conditions meant my body had to respond differently and overall I got fitter in a wider range of conditions - if you try to keep conditions the same your body gets used to it and you can be fit but in only a small range of conditions - or at least that's what I was taught

replies.author: @kom2falcon
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering All good.  And yes to "get fit" make life as hard as possible.  To win the race leave as much as possible for the end.  :)  Continued success.

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Entertaining as always :)
Seems to me, towards the end of the video you've proposed an answer to the question of "why buy that?" yourself: it's because they "think it's awesome", just like you think a bike made from paper is awesome, (so do I, BTW:).
The main distinction here is SPEND vs. WASTE.
We can spend our precious time on making awesome stuff, or spend it working for money, so that we can buy awesome stuff that others have made.
In both cases, we should all be careful not to WASTE our resources.

A final thought: any "awesome" is a good enough reason to SPEND our resources.
After all, GOALS ARE JUST AN EXCUSE TO WALK SPECIFIC PATHS.

Thank you for the time spent to make this video.

replies.text: nice points mate

author: @TerryGilsenan
text: Actually, when I rode seriously, I found my physiology reacted best to a very narrow range of RPM on the pedals. I then used the gears to match the bike and environment to that RPM, and I could (and did) ride for hundreds of KM without letting up. If I was forced outside that RPM sweet spot, the work mix on the various muscle groups changed and this impacted my stamina in a huge way.

replies.author: @RaperJason
replies.text: Exactly right I was told by a very old profesional cyclist that you should match your pedal stroke to your heartbeat using the gears it seems to work for me

The 27 speed I last had was virtually seamless and not expensive. A CVT would barely be an upgrade in terms of smoothness.

author: @spacecase0
text: The limit of the gear sets like you are holding in your hand is all about range. There's a limit to how large one year can be from the next before the chain won't properly move from one to the other. This is why you need the other set of 2 or 3 gears on the pedals to be able to take advantage of the larger range. It's not like someone needs 27 gears, but they certainly need the range offered by the combination of the two of them. If you live in flat land in a city you don't need that kind of range if you live in the Hills you don't need that kind of range, but if you are selling bicycles to both of those people there is no other option but to offer that sort of range

author: @johndough8115
text:
Sorry, but this was one of the Dumbest rants Ive heard in a long time.  I used to own both a single speed, and a 10 speed, when I was a kid, and I put so many miles and abuse on my 10 speed, to cause the tire to pop.  Years later as an Adult, I got a hybrid bike.  A cross between a road and mountain bike.  Pretty sure it was a 18 or 21 speed.

 I live in a city, that has a number of large hills.  They are not all steep hills.. but any decent graduation over a long period of time.. will quickly wear you down.   Having the lower tier gears easily allows you to peddle up small steep hills, as well as not having to hop off of the bike on lower grade hills that you would otherwise be too tired to peddle up.

 But here is the real issue...  If you are trying to peddle a much heavier Trike... then you are going to want as many gears as possible.  You will want to be able to accelerate that Trike ASAP,  from a parked stop at a red light that just turned green.  You also will want that acceleration, to avoid problems when a turning car, is about to crash into you.  And even more so... all of these hills that were a pain to deal with.. on a lower weight bike, will be much more straining with the increased weight of a trike.    In addition to wanting these lower gears to be able to keep from "Walking" your bulky trike up hills... you might also want larger gear ratios for maximum speed going on the straights or downhill.

 As for your comments about marketing... thats somewhat comical, considering that you are a Smoker.  Maybe you should consider getting off the cancer causing drugs... rather than trying to point fingers at useful engineering.

replies.author: @RaperJason
replies.text: Right on big steep Hills need very low gears and high ones on the down side anyway a 3speed sturmey archer is twice the price of an average 21 speed set

replies.author: @johndough8115

@AndreaDingbatt You seem far more Triggered than anyone else.  My point stands, that it was severely annoying to hear a 15min rant about how Gearing that makes it easier to peddle... is somehow a marketing ploy.  >_<

  As for the water... yeah, Im well aware of it.   Living in a basement, at age 48?  Nope.  Though, I had once been homeless for a year... living in my minivan.  Sucked balls.

 Ohh, and I have a certain hatred for Cigs... being that my mother was a Chain Smoker... and it made me sick to my stomach + made my clothing smell like a rotten skunk + took years off my life.  As a kid, you have no way to escape it.

 On the bright side... I recently heard that my mother got Cancer.  She totally deserves it.  Not just from chain smoking, and forcing me, my brother, and two sisters to smoke involuntarily ...but also, for all of the Psychological abuses she dished out on me in particular.  Hooray!

 I hope its a long, slow, extremely painful passing

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I rode seriously for the Halifax star wheelers and after a bit I ditched all of the gears and used to ride on a single freewheel and I lived in Yorkshire - the different conditions meant my body had to respond differently and overall I got fitter in a wider range of conditions - if you try to keep conditions the same your body gets used to it and you can be fit but in only a small range of conditions - let's face it - as I said in the video each to their own

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
It makes sense in competitive racing (were everyone else is using lots of gears too) as the rate of work per contraction affects the sustainable efficiency of muscles. Put simply, if the amount of fuel burnt in each power-stroke of the muscle exceed the rate at which lactic acid (a wast product of muscle metabolism) can be  processed, the acid  builds up so reducing the amount of fuel that can be burnt in the next contraction —  which also makes the muscles ache.  So competitive cyclists have relatively small muscle mass which rev the peddles quickly at low load levels but weight lifters have large muscle which just need to work in one short burst at very high loads. They develop different muscle cell types as well (slow twitch and fast twitch respectively). For lesser mortals, we can just peddle slower and lift pints a sip at a time. 
[These days when I find I’m in the mood for a jog, I simply lay down somewhere until the feeling passes.]

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Right.   ;-)
But it makes sense in a lot more than just 'competitive racing'.
'Av it!' Jack Lallane.

author: @jasonhaymanonthedrawingboard
text: It’s the tale of playing to something’s greatest strength. I wonder how far you could go with paper? I’ve seen canoes, especially one that was made over lockdown and posted on YouTube. Could you even make an airframe? A spaceplanes? Something that you could put together in a day. take a trip all the way off to the moon and back? Could Eco laminates be a solution to that? If you can make a bike with paper? I see no reason why the sky would be a barrier? Love what you doing here. Even a poor man with know how could do what your doing. Might take a while to gather materials.  Why I think its fantastic what your doing! Could even make a mini camper to go along with the bike made with a paper space frame? Perhaps a whole new industry? Elon musk might want a go? Your right about gear sets the more parts things has the more that can go wrong.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
The Nazis made their aircraft from paper..
Messershmitts, I think. 

Not that I'm fond of Nazis!
(* I Loathe them, my mother grew up in Nazi occupied Holland.)
Now I'm off for a couple of pints, I must apologise, I was about to bitch at you, because I had read your comment wrong!!
Nuance and the computer are not so great.
Anyway, my bad and I apologise unreservedly for being a twit, I am sorry.
Namaste 🙏
I hope you are well and have a great week ahead of you.
Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon. XxX.

replies.author: @jasonhaymanonthedrawingboard
replies.text: @AndreaDingbatt yeah I’m fine, I don’t think many are fond of nazis. What we have got to admit is when it came to the early years. the whole industrial complex aided in some of the advantages we see today. It is a shame they were demonically possessed! By a whole sick ideology. I hadn’t twigged about the nazi connection. So I guess in someways we are both twits. as I was focusing on construction rather than that back story.  It ok I miss read things too. It happens. We are all somewhat living inside our own minds and it can be hard to realise the world surrounding us.👍 frequently I’m having to see past a veil whenever it’s possible for me to do so. I will look into the Messerschmitt. As that something I was unaware of. I have an awful habit of oh is it a plane, train or superman? Without picking up on other details. I see a plane, it’s just a plane. I’m not really thinking about all the fundamentals that went into making the plane. It is more like I’m in my own bubble, and on the odd occasions. I do bump into something from the complete outside my conscious view of things. I re -evaluate what I know. It has an awful habit of catching me in a tangent and ramshackling my thinking at that moment in time. Complete catching me in a thought. With a million other thoughts. Can be quite disorienting. So yeah I totally get you. Namaste indeed🙏 may you find peace wherever the sun shines.  I didn’t get the original comment but I appreciate the epiphany. So namaste to you and all your friends.✌️👌

I can't find my lighter...

replies.author: @jasonhaymanonthedrawingboard
replies.text: @SoSinning paper mache is easily moulded. A graphite paper would have thermal resistance. There a intumescent paint that made from Burnt bone. NASA uses it on satellites. Starlite or at least it’s principles. react in the same way as paper mache does when exposed to flames or any heat source for that matter. Provided there wasn’t any loose fibres. I don’t think a lighter would do much. High temperature plasma arcs maybe. But that a push. I was thinking of a mono cockpit construction with a hull thickness of 8inches. That should be robust enough to withstand reentry forces. Provide the shell is reinforced with some embedded strand like material. Carbon fibre or glass or even a natural fibre braid. Every 2inches of thickness. Pretty sure it would be lighter then the space shuttle. By a country mile. Honey comb the stick into it in three layer alternating directions. By using a da Vinci bridge pattern on the inner hull. A straight pattern on the middle layer and spiralling around the outside.

author: @richysee
text: Hi there, seeing that you smoke like a chimney as did I, if you really, seriously want to quit as did I, I suggest that you trot along to your  doctor and get yourself a script for Zyban. It's an anti depressant that happens to interact with the brain's need for nicotine, purely as a side effect. In my case after taking the first tab I never had another cigarette . . . ever, even though I was smoking 40 odd per day rollies but with a filter. I had no unpleasant side effects and best of all I suddenly had pots of money at my disposal. Your doctor will  possibly know about Zyban and it's effects. The rest of your life is now up to you.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: Personally I don't think anyone should get involved in someone else's anti Airborne virus technology choices LOL

replies.author: @richysee
replies.text: @401ksolar I wrote that as a serious piece of information that can be used to save your own life if you happen to smoke. It's none of my business who smokes and who does not. Smoking cigarettes is almost impossible to stop having been there so I was very thankful when I was told about Zyban. All I'm doing is passing on valuable info and, if your knowledge of English was sufficient, you could understand that the information was intended as just that. Information.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text:
@richysee Sadly Zyban were not the answer for me...
I am oversensitive to ALL types of Antidepressant, and I suffer from depression...

They didn't tell me what they were, only they'd stop the cravings, I went totally Doolally, manic, suicidal and a bloody nuisance to my loved ones... 
And I craved them more than before,,, 

I am really pleased to hear that these were useful for you...
However, as with life in general, there is no one size fits all, and medication needs to be looked at by the individual and their GP.

I think you are most kind to offer this information, but I am fairly certain that Rob will be aware of them...

Bless you for trying to help, sometimes folks have already made a decision, like myself, where the stress, is far more likely to harm me, and cause me to be dead.....
(* I have underlying health issues.)
I hope you are well, and that you have a good week ahead of you. XxX.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @richysee yep sorry I speak the American dialect of English language so anyway, I try to shy away from receptor muncher type drugs from pharmaceutical companies that have a history of being on the list of medications for Mass shooters, but to each their own and glad your loved one survived your belt with the Pharma

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Glad it worked for you. I've seen Zyban work, I've also seen it go very wrong. My main concern is it targets specific receptor pathways and it's not always reversible if you stop taking it. If you actually can't stop smoking, even if you want to, either you are addicted or you have other issues; but some people like to smoke and can 'take it or leave it'. No one size fits all.
Peace.

Wheeee eee ee!!
Sorry, but I used to have the p*ss taken out of me, for my 2nd hand, 3geared bike!

So I used to lmao as I Cycled Past those lads with the bragging rights!!

Ahh, those were the days, Hehehe!!
Mind you, the old workhorse used to manage most terrain,, but my poor back could not...

* Quick brag and name drop,,,,
I beat Cptn Mark Philips on my mare with One Eye, she cost me £40.00.
Whereas His Horse was a Warmblood, that would have been about £8.000 and had been trained to perfection..

Difference was, he only rode the horse in competition, and it Didn't know nor Like him. 

Whereas, Dusty was my best friend, and we Trusted each other, a team.

His, sadly was 'something' to go compete, and Win On...

Sorry for that, but I miss the horses I worked with, not so much the people...

I've had a one of Those days,my best friend, service dog Jasper, has got Osteoarthritis in the stifles and has torn a cruciate ligament,, so my minds wandering,, - a lot..

Have a wonderful week ahead of you, and thank you for the answer, & patience as I Gibber.
Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon. XxX.

I hope you don't mind me, but I used to Fly past the lads, with Shimano, Expensive gears,. 

 Lmao, and the old R. Hercules and I had No Bother at All about it, I'd be laughing my head off overtaking....

And without much effort on my part, having done the 16miles to and from work...

Forgive an old Punk for having the Laugh about this, right up until I was 46yrs old!! 

I got Sooo much enjoyment from watching the lads sweating, huffing and puffing, legs gan ninty to the dozen,


    and getting ne-where so fast, I'm sorry, I have just put my back out, with the laughter!!
Oh deary me, yes, 

  Three gears and the local lads were terrified of me, doing their damndest to be away,, 
I have been Accused of using of using the broomstick... 🤣
Feck, it was so much fun!!

I hope you are feeling okay, please forgive me,, 
feel free to have a laugh at me putting my back out with laughter and I have mislaid me cigs!!

(* Not a senior moment, but my kids over - Medicated me on Purpose!!)

Thank you, and I hope you can understand, an old dear like myself, reliving her youth for a couple of minutes?!!
I couldn't see the Allure of more than 3gears, and I got the bike 2nd hand, when I was about 15 years old,right until I was 46yrs young, it was so easy to maintain.. 
Lol, but, I had to swap it for this damned wheelchair, Hehehee, but I can still remember the looks of admiration, I think I maybe got that wrong, and that it was looks of fury and spite.
Namaste 🤣🙏.
Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon, and Mr Dingbatt. XxX

author: @kdenyer1
text: When I had a bike with a lot of gears. Found only reason for ones between high and low was to give chain something to run up and down.

replies.author: @AndreaDingbatt
replies.text: LMFAO, Thank You!! 😏🤣. XxX

replies.author: @ScarletFlames1
replies.text:
Right, I use the big sprockets\gears or whatever they're called as a "am I going uphill or downhill" selector and the ones on the back wheel to account for the speed I am going at, if I already picked up speed and got to a hill I want to keep so I am on a medium gear on the small ones and a low gear on the central ones. When going from flat area to downhill I either change the gears for the condition at the bottom of the hill or if it's just a small decline then a high gear on both sets to allow me to pick up more speed.

I'd say my ideal gear range would be the normal sized low gear and a slightly upsized medium one with no need for the 3rd on the central gears and only 5 gears on the back but with the same total range of gearing.

author: @deanc6539
text: Come on. Tell us what you really think! Good waffle there.
